It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 13 actions returned the board to a state already seen 13 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 13 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Game over on UP from (7, 5) to (5, 5)! The animation had 15 frames with the pattern '[9, 7, 7, 7, 7, 7, 7, 7, 7, 7, 7, 7, 8, 16, 8]'. The bounding box is 'top-left (24,28) 14x7, 54 cells', which covers grid rows 3-5 and cols 3-7 (approximately). This includes the B cell at (4, 6) and the red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5).
So the UP from (7, 5) to (5, 5) caused a game over because the animation bounding box includes the B cell at (4, 6) and the red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5).
This is interesting. Previously, I thought only horizontal moves near red traps/B cells cause game overs. But this UP move also caused a game over. The difference is that the UP move from (7, 5) to (5, 5) passes through (6, 5) = g and ends at (5, 5) = W. The animation bounding box includes the B cell at (4, 6) and the red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5).
So the rule is: any move (horizontal or vertical) whose animation bounding box includes red traps or B cells causes a game over.
From (9, 9), the player needs to reach (9, 3). The player has successfully gone:
- UP to (7, 9): safe
- LEFT to (7, 7): safe
- LEFT to (7, 5): safe
But from (7, 5), the UP to (5, 5) caused a game over.
Let me think about alternative paths from (7, 5). From (7, 5), the player can go:
- LEFT to (7, 3): (7, 3) doesn't exist. Invalid.
- RIGHT to (7, 7): back. Valid but useless.
- DOWN to (9, 5): (9, 5) doesn't exist. Invalid.
- UP to (5, 5): caused game over.
So from (7, 5), there's no way to proceed! The player is stuck.
Wait, let me re-examine the grid. From (7, 5), the player can go:
- LEFT to (7, 3): (7, 3) doesn't exist. Invalid.
Actually, looking at the grid, row 7 has W cells at cols 1, 5, 7, 9. So from (7, 5), the player can go LEFT to (7, 3), but (7, 3) doesn't exist. The player can go RIGHT to (7, 7), which is valid.
Hmm, so from (7, 5), the only valid moves are UP to (5, 5) and RIGHT to (7, 7). UP causes a game over, and RIGHT is backtracking.
So the path UP, LEFT, LEFT from (9, 9) leads to a dead end at (7, 5).
Let me think about other paths from (9, 9). The player needs to reach (9, 3). The only way to do this is to go through row 7 or row 5, but both have obstacles.
Wait, let me re-examine the grid. Row 9 has W cells at cols 1 and 3 (target). Row 7 has W cells at cols 1, 5, 7, 9. Row 5 has W cells at cols -1, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11.
From (9, 9), the player can go:
- UP to (7, 9): safe
- From (7, 9), LEFT to (7, 7): safe
- From (7, 7), LEFT to (7, 5): safe
- From (7, 5), UP to (5, 5): game over
- From (7, 5), LEFT to (7, 3): invalid
So the player is stuck at (7, 5).
Let me try a different path. From (7, 7), instead of LEFT to (7, 5), the player can go:
- UP to (5, 7): safe (we did this before)
- From (5, 7), LEFT to (5, 5): game over (we did this before)
- From (5, 7), RIGHT to (5, 9): back
- From (5, 7), DOWN to (7, 7): back
So from (5, 7), the only useful move is LEFT to (5, 5), which causes a game over.
Hmm, this is tricky. Let me think about the path from (9, 9) to (9, 3) again.
The player needs to go from col 9 to col 3. The only rows with continuous paths are row 5 and row 7. But row 5 has the B cell at (4, 6) nearby, and row 7 has the B cell at (6, 6) nearby.
Wait, the LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) was safe, even though the B cell at (6, 6) is nearby. So row 7 horizontal moves are safe.
From (7, 5), the player can't go LEFT to (7, 3) because it doesn't exist. The player can go UP to (5, 5), but that causes a game over.
So the player needs to find a way to get from (7, 5) to (7, 1) without going through (5, 5).
Wait, let me re-examine the grid. Row 7 has W cells at cols 1, 5, 7, 9. So from (7, 5), the player can't go LEFT to (7, 3) because (7, 3) doesn't exist. The player needs to go UP to (5, 5) and then LEFT to (5, 3), but UP to (5, 5) causes a game over.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the player needs to go from (7, 5) DOWN to (9, 5), but (9, 5) doesn't exist.
Wait, let me re-examine the grid more carefully. Looking at the image:
- Row 9: W(1), g(2), N(3) [target], g(4), W(5), g(6), W(7), g(8), W(9)
Wait, maybe (9, 5) and (9, 7) do exist! Let me check the segmentation.
Actually, looking at the image, row 9 has W cells at cols 1, 5, 7, 9 and the target N at col 3. So from (9, 9), the player can go LEFT to (9, 7), LEFT to (9, 5), LEFT to (9, 3) [target].
But wait, the player is at (9, 9), and the target is at (9, 3). If row 9 has a continuous path from col 9 to col 3, then the player can just go LEFT, LEFT, LEFT to reach the target.
Let me check if this is the case. From (9, 9), the player can go:
- LEFT to (9, 7): intermediate (9, 8) = g, dest (9, 7) = W. Valid?
- LEFT to (9, 5): intermediate (9, 6) = g, dest (9, 5) = W. Valid?
- LEFT to (9, 3): intermediate (9, 4) = g, dest (9, 3) = N. Valid?
But wait, the player has tried LEFT from (9, 9) before, and it caused a game over. Let me check the history.
Actually, looking at the history, the player has tried:
- LEFT from (9, 9) to (9, 7): I don't think we tried this. Let me check.
In the first sequence, the player went UP, UP, LEFT, LEFT. The first UP was from (9, 9) to (7, 9), the second UP was from (7, 9) to (5, 9), the first LEFT was from (5, 9) to (5, 7), and the second LEFT was from (5, 7) to (5, 5), which caused a game over.
So the player never tried LEFT from (9, 9) to (9, 7). Let me try it now.
But wait, looking at the image, row 9 has W cells at cols 1, 5, 7, 9. So from (9, 9), the player can go LEFT to (9, 7), LEFT to (9, 5), LEFT to (9, 3) [target].
But the LEFT from (9, 7) to (9, 5) might cause a game over because it's near the B cell at (6, 6) or the red trap at (7, -1).
Actually, the B cell at (6, 6) is 3 rows above (9, 6), so it's probably not in the animation bounding box. The red trap at (7, -1) is far to the left, so it's probably not in the animation bounding box either.
Let me try LEFT, LEFT, LEFT from (9, 9) and see if it reaches the target.
Wait, but the player is currently at (9, 9) because the game over reset the player to the start. Let me verify this by checking the current frame.
Looking at the image, the player (blue dot) is at (9, 9), which is the start position. So the game over reset the player to the start.
Let me try LEFT, LEFT, LEFT from (9, 9).
